sentences_input.text: We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the SS scooped it up, but we managed to save a few and it was circulated in the various barracks.
And needless to say, it gave us some encouragement, because it gave us an idea how close the Allies are towards us.
However, the situation became more-- more adverse, because the population of the camp started to increase very rapidly.
They started to bring in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people from other camps.
And while this camp originally held about 20,000, 25,000 people, it rapidly increased to 40,000 to 50,000 to 60,000, to as high as the rumor was that there are 120,000 people now in the camp.
So it was a heavy, heavy load.
They couldn't feed us.

So that little bit of coffee that they gave us in the-- in the morning, around 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock after-- after it depends when the appell was finished, that either didn't come at all, or it came in-- in the afternoon.
And similarly with the turnip soup, either it-- it didn't come anymore at 1 o'clock.
It came either 4 o'clock in the afternoon or 6 o'clock at night or 12 o'clock midnight, and-- or it came the following morning at 4 o'clock in the morning.

And the famine was so huge that I remember those faces that, instead of going to lie down and-- and just stretch out, they would be leaning against the pillars of the-- wooden pillars of the-- of the barrack with that little can or that little dish that they may have had, waiting for that little bit of soup to come in.
And incidentally that little can or that little dish that they had, if they lost it or somebody stole it from 'em, then that was the finish.
And similarly if somebody got dysentery, that was also the finale.
That was it.
And people were dying on daily basis.
And it-- it was no longer-- you know, you didn't even pay too much attention.
You just piled them up like a stack of wood.
And then the wagon that would come and pile them up, you know, just throw them out and take them into the crematorium.
That was it.
It was a common sight that happened twice daily, so it wasn't frightening.
Now, we were adjacent to the stalag that contained the Dutch Jews.
And they were-- they were treated similarly to us.
They also had their own clothes on.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- [SIGHS] in March-- from-- in the last three weeks of March of 1944-- 1945, pardon me. 1945 was most crucial, because people were dropping dead by the-- by the lot, you know.
They were dying and-- and you saw the wagon hauling away the corpses, you know, in-- in a more frequent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] period.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Towards the end of March, I believe it was around March 27, 28, it was Pesach, I remember that, when we-- all of a sudden we were ordered that the younger people have to report to work to go and clean out some cisterns that were located near the kitchens, because they-- during the winter, they gathered an awful lot of grime and so on.
So we were-- we were given the orders to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to pump out with the water from those cisterns and to clean out those basins.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So 40 people were selected from our barrack.
And we were taken out.
It was the first seder night, was supposed to be the first seder night.
We were taken out, it was a horrible rain and cold.
And it was a hand pump that we had like 10 people on either side that we were pumping out.
And there were tons of water in that-- in that cistern.
And then every so often, every half an hour, they would change and then the other 20 would come in.
And no food, no nothing.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were the kapos that were with us.
And I remember there was that Greek Jewish kapo that was over us.
And he was just commanding us how to pump the water, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2.
And this was going on all night.
We were-- we were practically dropping off our-- of our feet.
We were adjacent to the kitchen.
And we were searching-- while we were relieved from one shift to the other, we were searching for some food.
And I found some-- in one corner I found some turnips and some onions, rotten onions it was.
So obviously we grabbed at anything that we could stuff in our mouth.
